title: Use PascalCase for named placeholders
description: Use PascalCase for named placeholdersAnalyze your codeconsistency-conventionalmaintainabilityCode SmellloggingWithin amessage templateeach named placeholder should be in PascalCase.Why is this an issue?How can I fix it?More InfoUsing consistent naming conventions is important for the readability and maintainability of code. In the case of message templates, using PascalCase for named placeholders ensures consistency with structured logging conventions, where each named placeholder is used as a property name in the structured data.The rule covers the following logging frameworks:Nuget package -Microsoft.Extensions.LoggingNuget package -SerilogNuget package -NlogAvailable In:Catch issues on the fly,in your IDEDetect issues in your GitHub, Azure DevOps Services, Bitbucket Cloud, GitLab reposi
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant: logger.LogInformation("User {userName} logged in from {ipAddress}", user, ip);
compliant: logger.LogInformation("User {UserName} logged in from {IpAddress}", user, ip);

checks.message: Use PascalCase for named placeholders in structured logging message templates

logic.detection_conditions: Logging method call with non-PascalCase message template placeholders
logic.triggering_conditions: AND: (1) Call to structured logging method (LogInformation, LogWarning, etc.) AND (2) Placeholder name starts with lowercase letter
logic.reasoning: Structured logging frameworks like Serilog, NLog, and Microsoft.Extensions.Logging use message templates where placeholders become property names. PascalCase convention ensures consistency with .NET naming standards and better readability in log aggregation systems. Non-PascalCase placeholders are detected by looking for lowercase first letter in placeholder names.
